name: Whiro
description: Whiro is a significant figure in Māori mythology, often depicted as the personification of darkness, evil, and death. He is considered the antithesis of Tāne, the god of forests and light, and their rivalry is central to many Māori creation stories. Whiro's origins are complex, sometimes described as being born alongside Tāne but choosing a path of darkness and destruction. He is associated with the underworld, called Rarohenga, and is believed to dwell there, plotting against the world of light. Whiro is attributed with introducing disease and suffering to humanity, and his influence is seen in negative aspects of the natural world, such as decay and rot. He possesses great power, capable of manipulating shadows and influencing the minds of mortals. While often portrayed negatively, Whiro also represents the necessary balance in the universe, as darkness complements light, and death is a natural part of the cycle of life. Some interpretations view Whiro as a force of chaos and disorder, constantly seeking to undermine the order established by Tāne and other gods. His actions are often motivated by jealousy and resentment towards Tāne's success in separating Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother). Whiro remains a complex and multifaceted figure, embodying the darker aspects of human nature and the constant struggle between good and evil within the Māori worldview.
subtitle: God of Darkness, Evil, and Death

keyMyths.title: The Rivalry with Tāne
keyMyths.description: Whiro and Tāne are eternal rivals, representing darkness and light. Their conflict shapes the world and defines the struggle between good and evil.

keyMyths.title: Whiro's Descent into the Underworld
keyMyths.description: Whiro is said to have descended into Rarohenga, the underworld, to plot against Tāne and humanity. He became the ruler of this realm of shadows and death.

keyMyths.title: The Introduction of Disease
keyMyths.description: Whiro is blamed for introducing disease and suffering into the world. This act is seen as a consequence of his jealousy and desire to corrupt Tāne's creation.
keyMyths.source: Orbell's Concise Encyclopedia of Maori Myth and Legend
